items.rationale: Seen as the most powerful crypto primitive of the decade — unlocks privacy + verifiable scaling; Lasso/Jolt are viewed as a new zkVM paradigm, and the team wants ZK brought to Bitcoin to make it programmable.
items.age_note: consistently restated 2022-08 through 2023-11; intensifying, not reversed

items.rationale: L2s are the scaling story; their key value is permissionless architectural innovation on top of Ethereum, and danksharding/DAS make L2 data cheap enough for new app classes.
Crypto can crowdsource ML compute/data and counterbalance big-tech compute advantage; framed as machines that can own and coordinate.
Treated as a prerequisite for mainstream adoption and for broadening DeFi/identity/gaming utility; default transparency seen as a hard ceiling.
Community-built IP is viewed as a surprise breakout use case; onchain remix/attribution graphs let creators capture downstream value — 'multiplayer for IP'.
True player ownership of in-game assets plus composability (assets as building blocks anyone can build on) is seen as the next frontier of game design.
Open, user-owned social graphs and messaging avoid web2 platform 'rug' risk; portability/interoperability of follow graphs is the missing piece.
Where verification cost is below incumbent margin, blockchains can build decentralized compute/energy/wireless/transport networks.
Belief that infra is under-built relative to apps and that most ambitious app ideas are compute-constrained; rails for human coordination are a massive opportunity.
Tokens treated as 'value vectors,' not equity — a tool to align incentives, distribute risk/reward and decentralize governance, far larger than a cap-table.
Merge-staking lets new middleware borrow Ethereum's economic security, de-leveraging systemic risk and lowering the cost of bootstrapping new services.
items.age_note: deep thesis stated 2022-08; lightly restated 2023-02/05, not heavily re-argued recently

items.rationale: User ownership weakens classic data/aggregation moats but creates a new 'community cohesion' network effect tied to assets; bigger pie even with shared value.

The dominant house concern: the SEC is seen as deliberately fostering uncertainty to gain leverage and effectively ban the tech, driving developers offshore; framed by late-2023 as a national-security issue and a lead the US is losing to China.
items.age_note: escalating steadily 2022-2023; most intense and most recent concern

items.rationale: MEV has moved to the center of protocol design and is treated as a tax on users; it breaks EIP-1559 incentive-compatibility and motivates new ordering/PROF/encryption designs.
Rollups fragment liquidity/interoperability and isolate apps; shared sequencers reintroduce a monopoly on ordering (censorship, rent, MEV capture) — a centralization risk.

Research shows liquidity flees the 'least safe' stablecoins under stress and LPs pull liquidity exactly when needed; endogenous/algorithmic collateral (Terra) is the root failure, not 'algorithms' per se.
Smart-contract dev is 'the opposite of agile' and still insecure; ZK circuit bugs are hard to scrutinize (only the proof is shared); some deployed SNARKs run at too-low security (FRI at 80 bits).
The 2022 collapses (FTX, Celsius, Voyager) are framed as failures of centralization/custody, not crypto; custody is 'a liability' and trust must be regulated, trustless protected.
Liquid staking concentration (Lido controlling >1/3 of staked ETH) and validator/client centralization raise governance-attack and consensus-safety concerns.
Entityless DAOs can't pay taxes, protect members, comply, or defend in court; courts/regulators may impose unlimited-liability partnership status (Ooki); attacks on DAOs are growing.
Applying a single licensing regime to exchanges and video games punishes developers for giving gamers true ownership and threatens decentralization via broad 'control' definitions.
Constant-product LPs bleed value to arbitrage (loss-versus-rebalancing), and over-supplied concentrated (V3) pools systematically lose money — a structural design problem mitigated by faster blocks.
A capable quantum computer would forge signatures, fork PoS, and advantage quantum miners; defenses exist but post-quantum signatures bloat state — a ~15-year watch item.

The house's central long-term bet: post-Merge a superior, high-economic-security base layer securing a large asset economy, with the richest developer ecosystem and composability, scaling via L2s + danksharding.
items.age_note: bullish through the 2022 Merge, sustained into 2023

items.rationale: Cited as the working example of effective token governance and of safer exogenous collateral; explicitly contrasted as lower-risk than Terra.
Respected as a technically superior 'better gold' with deep security and faith; but enthusiasm is conditional — the team wants ZK/programmability added and flags a long-run fee-only security problem.
items.age_note: appreciation steady; security-model concern is the open caveat (2023-07)

items.rationale: Flagged for opaque, lower-transparency reserves; under stress the market both flees to and from it on reserve-quality and regulatory-arbitrage grounds — a recurring run-risk node.
The canonical cautionary failure: undercollateralized with endogenous collateral, prone to death spirals; repeatedly invoked as 'not decentralized' and the model to avoid.
items.age_note: collapsed 2022; now cited as a permanent negative exemplar, not a live position

risk_regime.rationale: Through a bear market the house posture is structurally bullish on the technology and tells founders to keep building / stay the course ('still very early'; bear markets 'prolong the good faith innovation phase'). The conviction is on long-horizon decentralization tech, not a near-term price call — and it is heavily hedged by an acute, escalating US-regulatory overhang (SEC, offshoring, national-security framing) that is the single biggest downside they name. Net: long-term risk-on, near-term cautious on US-exposed/centralized exposures.

notes: Corpus is dominated by a16z crypto principals across distinct lanes: Miles Jennings/Dave Kerr (legal/regulatory — the loudest and most recent voice), Scott Kominers (NFT/IP/market design), Eddy Lazzarin & Chris Dixon (infrastructure/theses), and academic researchers (Boneh, Thaler, Roughgarden, Babai-style consensus/ZK talks). Treat 'sectors' as where the house places conviction; explicit forward token calls are rare — the corpus is thesis- and policy-driven, not a trade book, so token entries are necessarily thinner and partly inferred from thematic framing. A defining cross-cutting belief (near-axiomatic, every-month restated) is 'regulate apps, not protocols' and that decentralization changes an asset's risk profile under Howey — this underpins both their regulatory concern and their bullishness on genuinely decentralized systems. Recency handling: the SEC-as-existential-threat view sharpened materially over 2022→2023 (from 'guidance needed' to 'endgame is to ban'); the algorithmic-stablecoin stance is nuanced not reversed (anti-Terra/endogenous-collateral, pro decentralized/exogenous like DAI); EigenLayer/restaking is a strong 2022 thesis only lightly restated since, hence marked persisted. Conviction scores are intensity, not probability.
